title: SQL Injection in Order By
description: An endpoint that allows sorting results based on a user-provided column name.
vuln_code:
sort_column = request.args.get('sort', 'name')
query = f"SELECT id, name, price FROM products ORDER BY {sort_column}"
results = db.execute(query)

stages.name: Find the vulnerability
stages.instruction: Why is this vulnerable and why can't we use standard placeholders (?) for the column name?
stages.target: The column name is directly put into the query. Standard placeholders often only work for values, not for identifiers like table or column names.

stages.name: Exploit it
stages.instruction: Provide a payload to extract the database version using a time-based delay (assume PostgreSQL).
stages.target: (CASE WHEN (SUBSTR(version(),1,1)='P') THEN name ELSE id END)

stages.name: Patch it
stages.instruction: How would you fix this using an allowlist?
stages.target: Check if the 'sort' parameter is in ['name', 'price', 'id']. If not, default to 'name'.

stages.name: Explain it
stages.instruction: Why is input validation (allowlisting) crucial when you cannot use parameterized queries for certain parts of a SQL statement?
stages.target: Allowlisting ensures that only known-safe values are used, preventing an attacker from injecting arbitrary SQL commands or identifiers.

hints: Column names cannot usually be parameterized.
Think about using a CASE statement in the ORDER BY clause.
An allowlist is a list of allowed values.
